notes: If brethren dwell together, and one of them die, and have no child, the wife of the dead shall not marry without unto a stranger; her husband's brother shall go in unto her, and take her to him to wife, and perform the duty of an husband's brother unto her. And it shall be, that the firstborn which she beareth shall succeed in the name of his brother which is dead, that his name shall not be put out of Israel (Deut. 25:5-6). The situation in Ruth differs from the "levirate law" codified in Deuteronomy in two important respects: (1) Boaz was clearly not a brother-in-law (Hebrew yabam, Latin levir), and (2) there seems to have been no obligation for either Boaz or "Such a One" to marry Ruth, or it would certainly have come to their attention soon after the arrival of Naomi and Ruth from Moab. However, in one way-which is important for our appreciation of "Such a One's" reaction to Boaz' declaration of his intention to marry Ruth-the remarriage of a childless widow had the same effect as the "levirate": any offspring born to that union would have the right to inherit the property redeemed from Naomi. "Such a One's" initial willingness to redeem the land probably stemmed from his expectation that it would eventually pay for itself. However, the prospect of creating an heir to that property through Ruth, which heir might also be eligible to inherit the property that "Such a One" already owned and would otherwise have fallen to the sons he already had, made the purchase far less appealing. As a consequence, he encouraged Boaz to "redeem thou my right to thyself; for I cannot redeem it" (4:6b). Part of the storyteller's art lies in the suspense in which he kept his listeners for "Such a One's" reaction to Boaz' expression of intention to marry Ruth after "Such a One" had already said that he would redeem the property. Theology and Literary Characteristics The dramatis personae of the book of Ruth are unusual, if not unique, among the narrative writings of the Old Testament: in the entire story there is no king, no royalty, no priests, Levites, Judges, or prophets. All the characters in the story were ordinary people who lived in a town far removed from the centers of power, intrigue, and violence. A similarly striking feature of Ruth is its absence of traditional manifestations of Israelite religion: there is no evidence of the priesthood, nor is there a trace of oracles, visions, dreams, or any of the other usual means of determining God's will. Still, God's "all-causality" was felt continually as he moved in the background of events in the lives of everyday people who possessed the virtues of patience, loyalty, generosity, and trust. It brings the story to its successful and happy resolution. Related to the theme of God's "all-causality" is God's love, which extends to the lives and homes of the simplest of his people, as well as across national borders to traditional enemies of Israel. Ruth is repeatedly referred to as "the Moabitess" in the course of the story (1:22; 2:2, 6, 21; 4:5, 10). Yet, despite the fact that she was a foreigner, and despite the traditional antagonism between Israelites and Moabites (cf. Deut. 23:3-6), through God's love she was permitted to become a mother in Israel and to play an even larger role in God's design as the ancestress of David (4:22) and his greater Son Jesus (Matt. 1:1). Naomi left Bethlehem-Judah with a husband and two sons. When she returned to her home, she was widowed, bereft of both her sons; she brought only a Moabite daughter-in-law with her. Naomi lamented upon her return, "I went out full, and the Lord hath brought me home again empty" (Ruth 1:21). It seemed to Naomi that "the Almighty [had] dealt very bitterly" with her. Nevertheless God, in his never-flagging love, directed the subsequent events which led to the marriage of Ruth and Boaz and the birth of Obed, whom Naomi lovingly held in her bosom and for whom she acted as a nurse at the end of the story. Ruth is a masterpiece of the storytellers' art. With an absolute economy of words, without the use of a "villain" or any real antagonist, without the entrance of kings or priests onto the stage of the story, without the searing emotions or wrenching conflicts of which many stories are made, the teller of Ruth maintains the attention and interest of the listener from beginning to happy conclusion. As long as ordinary people who demonstrate the simple virtues of loyalty, love, and generosity are valued, the book of Ruth will remain a cherished part of our biblical heritage. (Stephen D. Ricks in Kent P. Jackson and Robert L. Millet, eds., Studies in Scripture, Vol. 3: Genesis to 2 Samuel, p.249-257)

reminded several times not to glean in any other field. This is again similar to God's requirement that we only come to him for our provision. Love underpins everything Boaz does as a prophetic example of God’s love underpinning everything he does for us. The Kinsmen Redeemer Ruth approaches Boaz as the kinsmen redeemer (goël) of Naomi and asks him to redeem her also through marriage. The Abrahamic Covenant was made between God and the children of Abraham through Isaac. We as Gentiles had no covenantal right to redemption. Just as Ruth was solely dependant upon the grace of Boaz or her redemption we are solely dependant upon the grace of our LORD for our redemption. Our part in that redemption is simply to avail ourselves of that grace. The threshing floor typology here is more subtle then many others in the book and one needs to understand what "threshing" is and what it is associated with prophetically. Now threshing is performed generally by spreading the sheaves on the threshing-floor and causing oxen and cattle to tread repeatedly over them (Deuteronomy 25:4; Isaiah 28:28). On occasions flails or sticks were used for this purpose (Ruth 2:17; Isaiah 28:27). There was also a “threshing instrument” (Isaiah 41:15; Amos 1:3), which was drawn over the corn. It was called by the Hebrews a (moreg) threshing roller or sledge (2 Samuel 24:22; 1 Chronicles 21:23; Isaiah 3:15). It was somewhat like the Roman tribulum, or threshing instrument. When the grain was threshed, it was winnowed by being thrown up against the wind (Jeremiah 4:11), and afterwards tossed with wooden scoops (Isaiah 30:24). The shovel and the fan for winnowing are mentioned in Psalm 35:5, Job 21:18, Isaiah 17:13. The refuse of straw and chaff was burned (Isaiah 5:24). Freed from impurities, the grain was then laid up in granaries till used (Deuteronomy 28:8; Proverbs 3:10; Matthew 13:30; Luke 12:18). Now prophetically the threshing floor and winnowing of the wheat is connected to the coming judgment of the LORD. (See Dan 2:35, Matt 3:12) We see it explained in Matthew 13:24-30. "Another parable put he forth unto them, saying, The kingdom of heaven is likened unto a man which sowed good seed in his field. But while men slept, his enemy came and sowed tares among the wheat, and went his way. But when the blade was sprung up, and brought forth fruit, then appeared the tares also. So the servants of the householder came and said unto him, Sir, didst not thou sow good seed in thy field? From whence then hath it tares? He said unto them, An enemy hath done this. The servants said unto him, Wilt thou then that we go and gather them up? But he said, Nay; lest while ye gather up the tares, ye root up also the wheat with them. Let both grow together until the harvest: and in the time of harvest I will say to the reapers, Gather ye together first the tares, and bind them in bundles to burn them: but gather the wheat into my barn." If Ruth is a type of the Church then it appears from this passage that the Church will be safely at the feet of it's Lord during the threshing. The threshing floor also has an amazing prophetic destiny. When the chaff is separated from the wheat as described in the historical notes above it is done at an elevated location with a prevailing wind. The saddle of a hill or mountain would make a perfect threshing floor. Jebus was the name of the Canaanitish city, which stood on Mount Zion (Joshua 15:8; 18:16, 28). It is identified with Jerusalem (Judges 19:10). It is also identified with the castle or city of David (1 Chronicles 11:4,5). It was a place of great natural strength, and its capture was one of David’s most brilliant achievements (2 Samuel 5:8). Jebusites is the name of the original inhabitants of Jebus, mentioned frequently among the seven nations doomed to destruction (Genesis 10:16; 15:21; Exodus 3:8, 17; 13:5, etc.). At the time of the arrival of the Israelites in Palestine the Jebusites were ruled by Adonizedek (Joshua 10:1, 23). Joshua defeated them, and their king was slain; but they were not entirely driven out of Jebus till the time of David, who made it the capital of his kingdom instead of Hebron. The site on which the temple was afterwards built belonged to Araunah, a Jebusite, from whom it was purchased by David, who refused to accept it as a free gift (2 Samuel 24:16-25; 1 Chronicles 21:24, 25). The temple was built at Jerusalem, on Mount Moriah, in the threshing-floor of Arnon (also spelled Araunah,) the Jebusite; where Abraham had offered up Isaac. This is where David met the angel of the Lord when he came with his sword drawn in his hand to cut off the people of Jerusalem for the sin,

The Book Of Ruth Introduction For me there are generally at least 3 levels of meaning in scripture. 1. Conveys a record of an event as it occurred with specific meanings for those specific people. 2. Contains more universal practical applications from the lessons taught in a particular book. 3. Prophetic. Many stories and events are types or foreshadows of future events. The book of Ruth is all 3 for me. I believe that Ruth, Boaz and Naomi as well as all the other characters mentioned are literal people who actually lived these experiences. I believe that there are universal moral and ethical implications in the book that are very deep and profound. I also believe that Boaz is a type of Christ (our kinsman redeemer), Naomi is a type of Israel (displaced from her land and inheritance but ultimately restored by Boaz). Ruth is a type of the church (She is a Gentile woman specifically excluded from the Abrahamic Covenant who is restored by becoming the bride of the kinsman redeemer). Historical Notes Up until about AD 450 the "Book of Ruth" was part of the "Book of Judges" in the OT when it was separated as a book. The period of the Judges is generally considered to be the time from Othniel until Samson. There is some argument on the exact dates the period is believed to be about 410 years and have ranged from about 1600 BC to about 1200 BC. This book was part of the time of the judges and most probably in the time of Gideon (see at Ruth 1:1). The fact that Ruth is a Moabite is essential to understanding the story so who exactly were the Moabites? The origin of both the Moabites and Ammonites is detailed in Genesis 19: 30-38 where Lot gets drunk and engages in sex with his all too willing daughters who both become pregnant. The animosity between the Israelites and the Moabites is throughout the OT. King David finally destroys that kingdom and takes great spoil from their country, which is ironic since through Ruth he is part Moabite. Deuteronomy 23: 3 has an interesting prophecy "23:3 An Ammonite or Moabite shall not enter into the congregation of the LORD; even to their tenth generation shall they not enter into the congregation of the LORD for ever:". Just how many generations existed between the sin of Lot's daughters and the marriage of Ruth and Boaz? Where a Moabite once again entered the congregation of the LORD through marriage? According to Genesis 11:27-31 Lot is Abraham's nephew so he is one generation removed from Abraham. If we turn to Matthew 1:1-5 we find 11 generations between Abraham and Boaz which makes 10 generations between Lot and Boaz when a Moabite woman was restored. Rahab the harlot is no doubt the same person as the Rachab mentioned in the genealogy of Jesus Christ, who married Salmon the tribe prince of Judah, to whom she bore Boaz, an ancestor of David (Matt 1:5). The genealogical proof of the descent of David from Perez through Boaz and the Moabitess Ruth (Ruth 4:18-22) forms not only the end, but also the starting-point, of the history contained in the book. Rahab was adopted into the congregation of Israel (Josh 6:25), and according to ancient tradition was married to Salmon (Matt 1:5), so the Moabitess Ruth was taken by Boaz as his wife, and incorporated in the family of Judah, from which Christ was to spring according to the flesh (see Matt 1:3,5) The story of Rahab is detailed in the book of Joshua Nothing more is known of Rahab. The accounts of the later Rabbis, such as that she was married to Joshua, or that she was the mother of eight prophets, and others of the same kind, are fables without the slightest historical foundation (see Lightfoot, hor. hebr. et talm. in Matt 1:5) When an Israelite is obliged to sell his inheritance on account of poverty, and actually did sell it, it was the duty of the nearest relation to redeem it as “goël”. But if it should not be redeemed, it came back, in the next year of jubilee, to its original owner or his heirs without compensation. Consequently no actual sale took place in our sense of the word, but simply a sale of the yearly produce till the year of jubilee (see Leviticus 25:10,13-16,24-28). When such a sale is made there is a sealed scroll written on the inside and out and sealed. Inside is the description of the title being transferred. On the outside is written the terms of the redemption that are to be performed by the one near of kin redeeming the property. We see

V. Events at the Gate (4:1-12) A. Boaz finds the nearer kinsman at the city gate and asks him to redeem Naomi's property and marry Ruth (4:1-5) B. When the nearer kinsman refuses to redeem the property and to marry Ruth, Boaz expresses a willingness to do so (4:6-12) VI. Marriage of Ruth and Boaz (4:13-18) A. Boaz and Ruth wed (4:13a) B. A son is born to Ruth and Boaz, whom Naomi's neighbors name Obed (4:13b-17) VII. Genealogy cited from Pharez (the son of Judah and an ancestor of Boaz) through Obed to King David Social Customs in the Book of Ruth The book of Ruth is rife with allusions to social customs and legal procedures observed at the time. An understanding of these customs will enhance an appreciation of the book. Among the most important of these are the references to gleaning in the fields (2:2-17), the redemption of property (4:3-7), and the remarriage of a childless widow. Gleaning. According to what must have been an ancient custom, codified in Leviticus and Deuteronomy, landowners were expected to leave the "corners" of the fields for the less fortunate members of the community to glean from. Thus in Lev. 23:22 we read: "And when ye reap the harvest of your land, thou shalt not make clean riddance of the corners of thy field when thou reapest, neither shalt thou gather any gleaning of thy harvest: thou shalt leave them unto the poor, and to the stranger" (cf. Lev. 19:9-10). Similarly, in Deut. 24:19 the Israelites were commanded: "When thou cuttest down thine harvest in thy field, and hast forgot a sheaf in the field, thou shalt not go again to fetch it: it shall be for the stranger, for the fatherless, and for the widow." Ruth, as a widow and a resident alien (Hebrew ger, rendered "stranger" in the KJV of Lev. 23:22 and Deut. 24:19), had a right to glean in the fields. It was a sign of Boaz' generosity (as well, perhaps, as a response to Ruth's request in 2:7: "I pray you, let me glean and gather after the reapers among the sheaves") that he told his workers to allow Ruth to glean throughout the field. That Ruth gathered an ephah (i.e., 29-50 pounds) of barley in one day attests to her success in gleaning. Redemption of Property and the Remarriage of a Childless Widow. At a pivotal point in Ruth, Boaz hailed someone called "Such a One" (Hebrew almoni peloni, something like "Mr. So and So") at the city gate and brought him before the elders of the city, who happened to be sitting there, and advised him of his opportunity to redeem the property of Naomi. At first "Such a One" seemed interested and expressed a willingness to purchase the property. At this juncture, however, Boaz informed him, "On the day that you purchase the field from Naomi, I will be acquiring Ruth the Moabitess, the wife of the deceased, in order to raise up a name for the deceased upon his inheritance" (4:5-my translation). This vital passage can best be understood in the light of the Israelite custom of redemption of property and the remarriage of a childless widow, practices which were designed to preserve property within the family and to perpetuate the memory of the deceased. The kinsman's right of redemption of property is outlined in codified form in Lev. 25:25-34. According to the custom in Israel, a kinsman had the right to redeem property that had been sold on account of poverty. The order of those having the right to redeem property may have been the same as the order for inheritance: brother, paternal uncle, and the "kinsman that is next to him of his family" (Num. 27:11). However, the situation in Ruth is somewhat different from the Leviticus legislation. In Lev. 25:25, because the property had already been sold, the stress was on the right of the kinsman to redeem it. In Ruth, the property of Naomi had not yet been sold, but the relative was given the right of first refusal. No obligation to do so is stated in Leviticus, nor is it implied in Ruth. Still, we must assume that this was the standard practice during the time the events recounted in Ruth took place, since, as D. R. G. Beattie pointed out, "a story-teller . . . will not create a legal situation which his audience will know to be impossible." The remarriage of a childless widow (codified in one particular form in the Pentateuch in legislation referred to as the "levirate law") is a practice mentioned in the twenty-fifth chapter of Deuteronomy:

quotes.text: ve expected the small book of Ruth to foretell such a great event? Ruth confidently met hardships not uncommon in our time-the death of a loved one, loneliness in a new place, and the need to work hard for her bread. Her small efforts, linked significantly to a later great event, tell me that each of us can take seriously the importance of our daily lives and decisions as we choose to follow God.
quotes.attr: Aileen H. Clyde, "Confidence through Conversion," Ensign, Nov. 1992, 89

quotes.text: There is only one exit gate leading unto eternal life. Unhappily, only a few find it—but not because God is exclusionary, but because they exclude God from their lives. Even God cannot bring to pass a reconciliation involving only one party.
quotes.attr: Neal A. Maxwell, in Conference Report, Oct. 1988, 40; or Ensign, Nov. 1988, 33
